core_claim: UnDefend is a denial-of-service and local privilege escalation tool that blocks Microsoft Defender definition updates or silently disables the antivirus. Released by Nightmare Eclipse as part of the June 2026 vulnerability wave.

slides.on_slide_copy: UNDEFEND KILLS DEFENDER AND LEAVES NO TRACE.
slides.subline: A dual-mode denial-of-service and privilege-escalation tool blocks definition updates and disables the antivirus silently. It is the third Nightmare Eclipse exploit.

slides.on_slide_copy: UnDefend is a dual-mode tool that can kill Defender on fully patched Windows.
slides.subline: No CVE assigned. The researcher proved persistent Defender blindness on Windows 10 and 11. This is exploit three of six from Nightmare Eclipse.

slides.on_slide_copy: It kills two specific services and leaves the antivirus completely blind.
slides.subline: Mode one freezes definition updates and cuts cloud lookups. Mode two stops the antimalware process with no notification and no event log entry.

slides.on_slide_copy: A kernel driver intercepts update traffic and silently drops every packet.
slides.subline: A second path writes a privileged registry key that disables tamper protection without a reboot, exploiting a Windows Filtering Platform callout driver flaw.

slides.on_slide_copy: Defender reports clean while its definitions are months out of date.
slides.subline: No alert fires. Event logs mark the service stop as expected. Cloud protection is already offline, and tamper protection is already gone.

slides.on_slide_copy: You have to verify Defender yourself because the status UI cannot be trusted.
slides.subline: There is no vendor patch yet. Alert when definitions exceed 24 hours old, confirm cloud connectivity is live, and watch for unexpected kernel driver loads.

caption:
Nightmare Eclipse released UnDefend as the third exploit in their June 2026 wave. A dual-mode denial-of-service and LPE tool that blocks Microsoft Defender definition updates or silently disables the antivirus entirely.

Two modes: (1) Update blockade — targets the Windows Update service (wuauserv) and Defender's cloud connectivity. Definitions freeze. Cloud lookups fail. The AV goes blind. (2) Silent disable — manipulates the tamper protection registry key via a privileged file write. Stops the Antimalware Service Executable (MsMpEng.exe). No toast notification. No event log entry.

The mechanism exploits a Windows Filtering Platform (WFP) callout driver flaw. Intercepts Defender's update traffic at kernel level. Drops packets silently. Second path bypasses tamper protection via registry manipulation. No reboot needed.

Defender shows green. 'No threats found.' 'Protection up to date.' But definitions are months old. Cloud protection is offline. Tamper protection is bypassed. No alert fires. Event logs show service stops as 'expected.' Defenders trust the green checkmark. The checkmark lies.

No vendor patch. Tamper protection can be bypassed. Defenders must verify Defender is actually working: monitor service health independently, alert on definition age exceeding 24 hours, validate cloud connectivity, hunt for WFP callout driver loads.

A green checkmark is not proof of protection. It is a UI state that can be decoupled from the engine.

How do you verify Defender is actually updating and running?

Follow for AI security breakdowns without the fake panic.

[UnDefend, Nightmare Eclipse, Windows zero-day, Microsoft Defender, denial of service, tamper protection bypass, WFP callout driver, definition updates, silent disable, endpoint validation]
comment_prompt: How do you verify Defender is actually updating and running?
alt_text: Title card: UnDefend silences Microsoft Defender with no update and no alert. A denial-of-service and privilege-escalation tool that blocks definition updates and disables the antivirus silently, the third Nightmare Eclipse exploit. A Defender shield's update pulse flatlines into a red line.

Nightmare Eclipse dropped UnDefend as the third exploit, with two modes: block definition updates indefinitely, or disable Defender entirely. No CVE, persistent blindness, working on fully patched Windows 10 and 11. A security panel reads fine while definitions sit at zero days old.

UnDefend targets the Defender update service and the Antimalware Service Executable to stop signatures, cloud protection, and the antivirus, either freezing definitions or stopping the service with no toast or event entry. Two service icons in the dark, both with red stop symbols.

It exploits a Windows Filtering Platform callout driver flaw to intercept Defender's update traffic in the kernel and drop packets silently, and a second path flips the tamper-protection registry key via a privileged write, with no reboot. A network packet dissolves into red dust before reaching Defender.

Defender shows green and reports no threats and up to date, but definitions are months old, cloud protection is offline, and tamper protection is bypassed, while no alert fires and logs mark the stops as expected. A green dashboard with a faint red data stream leaking unseen.

Defend by monitoring Defender service health independently, alerting when definitions are older than 24 hours, validating cloud connectivity, and hunting WFP callout driver loads. A split scene: a live shield with a cloud link versus a flatlined one with a red kernel driver beneath.

A green checkmark is not proof of protection. It is a UI state that can be decoupled from the engine. A Defender shield half glowing with a live pulse, half faded grey with a red kernel driver beneath.
The call to action asks how you verify Defender is actually updating and running. A monitoring view of Defender definition age and service status on a clean dark end card.

narration.text: A researcher named Nightmare Eclipse just dropped a tool called UnDefend, and it can kill Microsoft Defender without triggering a single alert.
It comes in two modes: one freezes definition updates indefinitely, and the other stops the antimalware process entirely, both working on fully patched Windows 10 and 11.

The first mode targets the Windows Update service and cuts off cloud lookups so signatures never arrive. The second mode writes a privileged registry key that disables tamper protection with no reboot required.
The underlying trick is a flaw in the Windows Filtering Platform callout driver, which lets the tool intercept Defender's update traffic at the kernel level and drop every packet before it lands.

Here is what makes this dangerous: Defender's interface still shows green, reports no threats, and claims protection is up to date, even while its definitions are months old and cloud protection is completely offline.

There is no vendor patch yet, so the practical fix is to verify Defender's health from outside its own status UI: alert when definitions exceed 24 hours old, confirm cloud connectivity directly, and watch for unexpected kernel driver loads.
A green checkmark is a UI state, not proof that the engine is actually running. Those two things can be completely decoupled from each other.
How do you verify that Defender is actually updating and running on your endpoints? Drop it in the comments.

beats.caption: UnDefend silences Defender. DoS plus LPE.
Dual mode. Update blockade. Silent disable. Patched hit.
wuauserv and MsMpEng targeted. No toast. No event log.
WFP driver drops packets. Registry bypasses tamper protection.
Green checkmark. Definitions old. Cloud dead. Checkmark lies.
Monitor health. Alert age. Validate cloud. Hunt drivers.
Checkmark not protection. UI state decoupled.
